nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef drop-offs and outer reef slopes, generally at depths of 10–60 m

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on zooplankton (copepods, amphipods). Tends to accept frozen mysis shrimp and enriched frozen brine shrimp; has been known to transition to high-quality marine flake or pellet foods over time. An attached refugium cultivating copepods and amphipods is generally advised to provide a steady live food supply, particularly during acclimation.

goodWith: other anthias species (with care during introduction)
avoidWith: predatory fish that eat small reef fish

summary: A colorful planktivorous anthias from the Indo-Pacific, commonly kept in reef aquariums. Generally considered suitable for intermediate keepers; tends to do best in small groups in spacious tanks with ample open water and a regular supply of frozen or live foods.
careNotes: Red Fairy Anthias are protogynous hermaphrodites — all individuals are born female, and in a group the dominant individual will transition to male. Males display a well-developed lateral red bar and a red caudal fin; females show red-tipped caudal fins and a pinkish-orange body. They have been known to jump from open aquaria, so a tight-fitting lid is generally advised. They are active, mid-water planktivores that require ample open swimming space; sources describe them as needing a large amount of room. A refugium producing live copepods and amphipods is widely recommended to supplement frozen foods, especially during initial acclimation. Also sold as 'Silver-Streak Goldie Anthias'. Sources disagreed on adult size: LiveAquaria cited 6 inches (15.2 cm) while BlueZoo Aquatics cited 4.5 inches (11.4 cm); the accepted range of 11–15 cm averages these figures. Sources also disagreed on minimum tank size (125 gal vs 70 gal); 500 L was selected biasing toward the larger value given the active swimming behavior noted by LiveAquaria and the need to house a group.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Protogynous sequential hermaphrodites — the dominant individual in a haremic group transitions from female to male. In a well-maintained harem in a very large aquarium, spawning has been documented, but raising pelagic larvae through settlement is extremely difficult.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, tank size, care level, temperament, reef compatibility, and native range. Size disagreement: BlueZoo 4.5 in vs LiveAquaria 6 in — averaged to 11–15 cm range. Tank size disagreement: BlueZoo 70 gal (265 L) vs LiveAquaria 125 gal (473 L) — selected 500 L biasing toward larger given active swimming behavior and group housing requirement.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous sequential hermaphrodite; rarely bred in home aquaria. Spawning has been observed in large systems but rearing pelagic larvae is extremely difficult.
